title: Salesforce Custom Object Bulk Delete and Data Destruction
dataSources: `index=salesforce` **`salesforce:event_log`** sourced from Salesforce **ELF REST** pulls (**hourly default**, **3600s** interval typical) requiring **Event Monitoring add-on / Shield**. This UC targets programmatic destruction paths **`EventType IN ("ApexExecution","API")`** with **`Operation="delete"`**, inspecting **`EntityName`**, **`EntityId`**, **`UserId`**, **`Username`**, **`ApiType`**, and correlation-friendly timestamps. Some orgs need supplemental **Real-Time Event Monitoring** or **Transaction Security** policies if delete semantics are partial—document coverage gaps. **`salesforce:login_history`** optional for operator attribution. **CIM note:** **No CIM**—use **custom threat objects** and backup vendor correlation instead of data model routing.
app: `Splunk Add-on for Salesforce` ([Splunkbase 3549](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/3549)), ≥5.0. Install on the Search Head (for field extractions and saved searches) and on a dedicated Heavy Forwarder if running data collection off-SH. The TA provides both collection and parsing. The TA requires a Salesforce Connected App with OAuth 2.0 credentials and an Event Monitoring or Salesforce Shield license.
spl:
index=salesforce sourcetype="salesforce:event_log" EventType IN ("ApexExecution","API") Operation="delete"
| stats count as deletes, dc(EntityId) as unique_records, values(EntityName) as objects by UserId, Username
| where deletes > 100 OR unique_records > 50
| sort -deletes
description: Surfaces mass delete operations executed through API or Apex execution paths by counting delete events and distinct record identifiers per user—calling out possible sabotage, insider cover-ups, or destructive automation run amok.
value: Bulk erasure can destroy evidence for financial crime investigations, breach customer contracts, and break retention duties; rapid alerts enable backup restores, legal holds, and regulator notifications before statutory windows close.
implementation: Normalize Operation and EntityName fields from your Event Monitoring mapping; align thresholds with largest approved data-quality jobs; integrate with Salesforce recycle bin and backup vendor APIs for corroboration.
visualization: **Row 1 —** Stacked bar chart of **`deletes` by `EntityName`** (click sets `$object$` token). **Row 2 —** Master-detail table (**`Username`, `deletes`, `unique_records`, multi-value `objects`**) with export. **Row 3 —** Sparkline trend (**`span=1d`**, default range **Last 30 days**) comparing daily delete volume vs 30-day median band. **Row 4 —** **Recycle Bin status** supplementary panel querying accelerated summary (or scripted REST output ingested separately) outlining recoverable counts for highlighted user.
dataModelAcceleration: Not applicable for the primary search: it relies on application- and industry-specific log formats, not a normalized CIM data model.

references.title: Splunk Add-on for Salesforce — Data Collection Configuration

knownFalsePositives: (a) **Legal retention / privacy erasure programs:** GDPR/CCPA-driven **right-to-erasure** batches produce high `deletes` volumes—**mitigate** require `privacy_ticket` references in `lookup retention_jobs`, Legal attestation, correlate DPO calendar, page only when job absent or object class outside approved scope. (b) **Sandbox refresh & scratch-org rebuild scripts:** destructive resets appear catastrophic—**mitigate** hard exclude non-prod org streams from prod alert macros while monitoring sandboxes under separate thresholds. (c) **Duplicate-merge & data-quality bots:** merge tools emit delete-like API counts—**mitigate** tag `MergeBot`/MDM service principals, match known maintenance windows, escalate when bot absent or object mix atypical. (d) **Customer churn / contract terminations:** contractual purge waves spike deletes—**mitigate** CRM lifecycle lookup tying `Account.Status=Closed Lost` with expected delete curves and RevOps sign-off. (e) **Broken ETL rollback jobs:** integration reversals replay deletes—**mitigate** dynamic ratio tests vs expected upper bound per integration, paging when deviation >3σ while stable integrations auto-suppress briefly pending engineering ticket. (f) **Nightly housekeeping integrations:** archive jobs trim history tables—**mitigate** per-service median baselines, ASN/IP correlation, rotating suppressions monthly with risk acceptance owner.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• **`Splunk Add-on for Salesforce` ≥5.0** on **Search Heads** (field extractions, saved searches, dashboards) and, for production hygiene, a **Heavy Forwarder** that performs OAuth-authenticated REST collection away from interactive admin sessions.
• **Salesforce entitlements & app security:** **Event Monitoring add-on** and/or **Salesforce Shield** for **Event Log File (ELF) REST** access; **Connected App** with **OAuth 2.0**; least-privilege integration user; credentials in Splunk **storage/passwords** or your secret manager—not cleartext in `local/inputs.conf`.
• **Indexes & RBAC:** route to **`index=salesforce`** (or `salesforce_prod` vs `salesforce_sb` macros); SOC and IAM analysts get **`srchIndexesAllowed`** without `admin`; restrict dashboard tokens exposing `Username`/`UserId` broadly.
• **Ingest economics:** default ELF poll **3600s**; pilot 7–14 days and forecast **GB/day**; trim unnecessary EventTypes if license pressure emerges; document `_indextime` vs `_time` skew monitoring for catch-up bursts.
• **Lookups & governance:** provision change-controlled CSV/KV lookups (allowlists, object classification tiers, integration registries); define **RACI** across CRM platform engineering, SOC, IAM, Data Owners, and Legal for escalations touching regulated datasets.
• **Extraction regression guard:** after each TA upgrade, run `fieldsummary` on the UC’s canonical fields for 10k recent events and archive results in Git to catch JSON path drift early.

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Enable `salesforce:event_log` inputs covering API and Apex categories. Key fields: EventType, Operation, EntityId, EntityName, UserId, Username, ApiType, _time. Validate that delete operations populate—some tenants require supplemental Platform Event feeds; document gaps.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run hourly with adaptive thresholds tuned per environment:

```spl
index=salesforce sourcetype="salesforce:event_log" EventType IN ("ApexExecution","API") Operation="delete"
| stats count as deletes, dc(EntityId) as unique_records, values(EntityName) as objects by UserId, Username
| where deletes > 100 OR unique_records > 50
| sort -deletes
```

Understanding this SPL
The search isolates programmatic delete-heavy pathways, aggregates volume and cardinality of touched records, and sorts by descending deletes to prioritize the loudest destructive actors—add exclusions for housekeeping service accounts via lookup tables.

Step 3 — Validate
Pivot to Salesforce delete audit views, recycle bin timelines, and backup diff reports. Confirm EntityName sensitivity (custom financial objects vs. chatter). Capture screenshots for SOC evidence packages when malicious intent is suspected.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Trigger playbooks that freeze offending credentials, initiate restore jobs, notify legal and compliance leads, and feed executive risk dashboards illustrating delete anomalies versus baseline maintenance windows.

Bulk delete and destructive operations — deepening resilience narratives
Deletes via **API** and **Apex** encompass both **hard deletes** and **Recycle Bin-phase** deletes depending on object and configuration nuances; investigative guidance must emphasize **Recycle Bin dashboards**, **Backup API deltas**, and **offline backup vendor** confirmations—Splunk seldom differentiates programmatically unless fields like **`IsHardDelete`** or contextual `EntityName` heuristics exist—verify per tenant instrumentation.

Operational split: malicious vs housekeeping
Maintain **`sf_delete_allowlist_accounts`** integrating expected nightly purge volumes; dynamic ratio tests (`actual/expected_upper_bound`). Sudden divergence triggers investigation while stable ratio suppresses benign housekeeping.

Cardinality nuance beyond dc(EntityId)
Some paths emit duplicate `EntityId` across batch boundaries—evaluate **`estimated_distinct`** caution; cross-check **`values(EntityName)`** diversity.

Destructive interplay with GDPR right-to-erasure
Legitimate bursts—coordinate with **`privacy_program_windows`** lookups containing legal-approved batch references.

Incident severity matrix
Elevate automatically when **`EntityName`** intersects **`regulated_core_objects`** or when deletes cluster within minutes of **`privilege_grant`** macros from UC‑10.12.48 (post-exfil cover-up heuristic—still probabilistic).

SPL variant counting duration spread
Wrap with **`bucket _time span=15m`** pre-stats to identify sustained campaigns vs impulses.

Purple team destructive dry run sandbox
Mandatory before production rollout of threshold changes affecting integration accounts.

Forensic sequencing
Maintain **`transaction`** `maxpause=30m` by UserId bridging multi-hour campaigns into single incidents for narrative clarity.

Operational correlation with CASB egress and DB logs
Deletes preceding large export alert (UC‑10.12.47) compound suspicion—implement macro chaining results into single investigation board.

Recycle Bin operational note
Splunk counts may omit soft-deleted state transitions undetectable if logging incomplete—explicitly instruct analysts to validate in Salesforce UI not only Splunk.

Quarterly KPI set
Deletes per million records baseline, divergence factors, recurrence of top offenders integration vs human.

Governance interplay with legal holds
Deletes under hold should theoretically fail—persistent alerts imply misconfiguration worthy of Sev1 platform incident separate from SOC narrative.

Adaptive thresholding seasonal effects
Quarter-end archiving projects—calendar integration.

Backup restoration readiness drills
Quarterly tabletop restoring subset sandbox mirroring catastrophic delete—not live customer data—for muscle memory.

Edge case: cascading deletes fan-out
Fan-out may amplify counts benignly—object relationship map aids interpretation.

Edge case: big object asynchronous deletes delayed logging
Lag between user action & log emergence— widen evaluation window after integration spikes.

Edge case: managed package housekeeping
Vendor accounts—whitelist with expiry discipline.

Edge case: partial API failure duplicates log lines
Dedup hashing earlier guidance.

Edge case: merge-induced pseudo deletes
Data stewardship merges— annotate expected counts.

Edge case: sandbox masking scripts
Excluded via org segmentation.

Operational metrics trend panel
Rolling 30d median deletes with confidence band.

Incident closure categories
Approved retention job, tooling bug, insider sabotage hypothesis, ransomware precursor (speculative escalate), erroneous threshold.

Environmental sustainability
Prefer summarization before brute force scans.

Correlation with infra DB deadlocks sporadic deletes
Rare cross-domain pivot.

Historical retrain after Apex logging level changes counts
Maintain TA notes.

Research horizon
Leverage Salesforce **Shield Event Monitoring** real-time deletes if economically justified.

FAQ regulator
Demonstrate segregation between detection and transactional prevention controls.

Operational fatigue management backoff
Backoff algorithm if housekeeping integration repeatedly crosses threshold legitimately pending architecture fix—track deferred risk acceptance ticket.

Quarterly KPI set additive
Deletes correlated with dormant account reactivation (possible compromise).

Executive summary metric
Annual destructive anomaly count trending down post SOAR integrations.

Correlation with phishing preceding credential theft timelines
Broad threat intel optional.

Quarterly tabletop destructive insider scenario fused with UC‑10.12.46 API spike prelude.

Environmental noise from data quality tooling
Duplicates cleanup waves—coordinate data governance calendar.

Portfolio synergy UC‑10.12.49
Deletes shortly after suspicious login geo anomalies compound risk macro.

Adaptive threshold logistic regression caution
Defer until oversight.

Historical evidence retention elongated for litigation holds possibly multi-year Splunk archiving policies must align.

Governance, evidence, and continuous control testing
Establish this detection as a named IT General Control for custodial CRM data. Each quarter, export a statistically significant sample of alerts (for example, 25 cases) and perform a **quality review** covering five dimensions: (1) timeliness relative to Salesforce poll latency, (2) accuracy versus ground truth in Salesforce admin screens, (3) completeness of ticketing metadata, (4) appropriateness of suppressions, and (5) analyst narrative quality suitable for regulatory interviews. Store evidence in your GRC repository with hashes and retention aligned to your legal hold policy. When Splunk or the Salesforce TA upgrades, repeat a **regression harness** that replays frozen JSON fixtures through a dev indexer to ensure field extractions remain stable. Maintain a **change advisory** record whenever thresholds, lookup schemas, or cron schedules change, because auditors will ask for a traceable history of tuning decisions.

Performance, workload, and search optimization
Salesforce security searches can become expensive if they scan wide time ranges without partition discipline. Prefer **index-time routing** that isolates production org data, and use **`tstats` only when you have a compatible data model**—which is rarely true for raw Salesforce JSON—so focus on **fast filters** (`sourcetype`, `EventType`, `index`) and **summary indexing** for baselines. On Splunk Cloud, monitor **search budget** consumption for the service account running these schedules; split heavy baseline jobs from alert jobs. For on-premises, place collection on a **Heavy Forwarder** to keep REST polling off search heads. If you enable **workload management**, assign these searches to a pool with guardrails so ad-hoc human searches do not starve operational detections.

Risk communication and executive narrative
Translate technical hits into business language. For each incident class, maintain a one-paragraph **executive summary template** describing customer impact, financial exposure, and remediation status. This elevates the UC from a SOC tool to a board-ready control narrative, especially in financial services where CRM data includes wealth, insurance, or mortgage artifacts. Pair metrics with **leading indicators** (integration onboarding rate, active connected apps) so risk committees can ask forward-looking questions rather than only reviewing rear-view alert counts.

Deep-dive operational walkthrough — destructive operations with restoration readiness stress testing
Destructive anomaly detection intersects incident response with **business continuity**. Start by defining numerical **risk bands** aligning Splunk thresholds to **Recovery Time Objective (RTO)** expectations for CRM data subsets. Critical customer contract objects merit lower numeric thresholds because backup restoration parallelism may saturate during widespread incidents—early detection buys hours.

Operationalize weekly **_restore readiness checks_** not only for backups historically but correlating Salesforce object families with restoration runbooks—including who authorizes restores, vault access processes, validation queries post-restore, and communication templates to customer-facing teams if partial data inconsistencies emerge after crash-consistent restores.

Establish **dual verification** between Splunk-derived delete spikes and Salesforce **Recycle Bin** state transitions when UI telemetry available via auxiliary ingest or scripted checks—disagreement implies logging gaps requiring platform engineering escalation, not SOC dismissal.

Maintain **scenario libraries** distinguishing malicious insider patterns (rapid deletes scattering across heterogeneous sensitive objects following privilege elevation) from integration housekeeping (narrow object scope, monotone cadence aligning with nightly schedules). Scenario libraries sharpen analyst instincts and onboarding speed.

Purple team drills should emulate **destructive automation bug** regressions—not only adversaries—because many impactful historical incidents arose from erroneous deployment scripts with valid credentials.

When investigating potential cover-up sequencing (exports then deletes—macro correlation across UC‑10.12.47 and this UC), treat timelines holistically rather than compartmentalizing tool alerts. Storyboards win prosecutions and regulatory confidence more than fragmented tickets.

Operational metrics should quantify **recovery exercise success rate** quarterly: percentage of practice restores completing within SLA with validated record counts—not merely Splunk suppression tuning metrics.

Environmental complexity: multitenant ingestion must guard against **sandbox destructive noise** falsely elevating production risk—macros excluding non-prod Organization IDs deserve quarterly audits ensuring new sandboxes routed correctly over time.

Finally, instill **ethical clarity**: accusing individuals of sabotage carries HR and legal ramifications—SOC language must remain investigative, hypotheses labeled, confirmations deferred to sworn evidence channels. Splunk empowers speed; humans remain accountable for restraint and fairness.

Supplemental continuity — quantified impact, customer notifications, and systemic remediation
When deletes appear malicious or grossly negligent, coordinate early with **Legal** to estimate **customer record exposure** using object relationship maps—not just raw Splunk counts—because contractual notification thresholds may depend on whether **PII-bearing child records** were orphaned or destroyed. Maintain a **quantified impact worksheet** template linking Splunk aggregates to business record definitions. For systemic issues (automation bugs), drive **root-cause engineering** items with acceptance criteria including added **pre-delete validation hooks** in Salesforce or integration layers, not only adjusted Splunk thresholds. Post-incident, publish an **internal blameless summary** highlighting detection value, restoration challenges, and backlog items to prevent repeat failure. Long-term, evaluate **event streaming** or **transaction policy** investments that can block destructive classes pre-commit while Splunk retains the forensic corroboration mission.

Final addendum — integration with enterprise backup SLA math and cascading risk scoring
Formalize mapping between Splunk-derived delete magnitudes and **backup vendor RPO/RTO** assertions so executives understand whether observed destruction exceeds recoverable horizons. Combine delete spikes with **identity risk posture** scoring (privileged users, dormant reactivations, recent MFA resets) inside a Splunk **summary risk index** powering a single investigative queue—avoid duplicate paging while preserving nuanced prioritization. Run **annual catastrophe simulations** where Splunk-derived signals inject synthetic mass-delete timelines into crisis management rehearsals, validating cross-functional choreography among CRM platform, SOC, Legal, Fraud, Communications, and Customer Support. Close with **risk acceptance registers** documenting any consciously retained residual exposure when architectural prevention lags—for example awaiting budget approval for realtime transaction guardrails—and ensure executives re-sign acceptance quarterly until remediated.

Closing operational framework — chaining detections and long-horizon learning loops
Treat delete-volume telemetry as one node within a broader **destructive-chain** analytic strategy. Schedule a nightly **retroactive correlation** saved search joining this UC’s outputs with privileged-change alerts (UC‑10.12.48), abnormal export bursts (UC‑10.12.47), risky API outliers (UC‑10.12.46), and authentication anomalies (UC‑10.12.49) inside a constrained `join`/`stats` envelope to flag **composite risk scores** without overwhelming analysts daily—surface only scores crossing executive-agreed escalation thresholds after multi-signal corroboration. Maintain a **lessons repository** tagging each destructive incident variant (automation regression, insider, compromised integration, ransomware precursor hypothesis) feeding semi-annual **control design reviews**. Where feasible, ingest **offline backup differential summaries** trimmed to aggregates (counts per object family) strictly for corroboration, honoring data minimization. Finally, socialize that Splunk alerting complements—not replaces—**Salesforce recycle bin safeguards**, **field history**, **big object archiving**, and **legal hold tooling**; success is measured jointly by prevented customer harm and demonstrable investigative speed, not alert counts alone.

Micro-addendum — documentation hygiene: archive Splunk search revision identifiers, TA build numbers, Salesforce API versions, and runbook edits together so auditors can reconstruct the exact analytic state underpinning each historical destructive-event investigation without ambiguous guesswork.

Continuity capsule — versioning and audit replay discipline
Maintain a changelog entry whenever Splunk SPL, lookups, Salesforce API versions, or TA builds change materially, linking to ticketing records and approval artifacts so third-line assurance can reconstruct the analytic posture for any historical timeframe without speculative interpretation.
Schedule as Alert: cron **`10 * * * *`**, range **`-75m@m` to `-5m@m`**, trigger **>0**, throttle **`UserId + EntityName` 6h**, dynamic thresholds per environment via macro `sf_delete_thresholds`, integrate **composite macro** with UC‑10.12.47/48 when same `UserId` within ±2h.

Step 5 — Troubleshooting
• **Symptom — Deletes not logged** — API logging gaps; enable supplemental monitoring or Platform Events; document residual risk.  
• **Symptom — Recycle bin conceals soft deletes** — instruct analysts to verify UI; Splunk counts may not equal hard delete.  
• **Symptom — Integration nightly job constant breach** — adjust baseline or fix architecture (batch size).  
• **Symptom — `EntityId` null** — widen stats to count events without cardinality filter or parse alternate id field.  
• **Symptom — Apex vs API mis-tagged** — expand `EventType IN` after JSON review.  
• **Symptom — False panic during sandbox refresh** — macro `sf_prod_only` mis-set; correct org filter.  
• **Symptom — Restore drill fails** — operational BC issue; route to backup team with Splunk timeline attachment.
grandmaExplanation: We ring the bell when someone erases piles of customer records all at once through the automated hooks of our database, because wiping huge chunks of history can hide theft, sabotage honest books, or break promises we made to regulators.
